subtopic_detail: Depression with psychotic features in older adults
stem: You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar reviewing a 76-year-old woman admitted under geriatrics with weight loss of 8 kg over 2 months, reduced oral intake and persistent low mood. She has nihilistic delusions that her bowels have rotted and refuses food because of this. She has tried sertraline at 100 mg daily for 8 weeks and mirtazapine at 30 mg daily for 6 weeks as outpatient with no response. She has no past history of psychosis. She has retained capacity and is willing to consider further treatment.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/68 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.5°C, weight 48 kg, BMI 18 kg/m^2
data_table.Examination: Psychomotor retardation, nihilistic delusions, MMSE 28 of 30, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH, B12, folate, calcium all within normal limits, ECG normal sinus rhythm with QTc 440 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in treatment?

options.text: Switch mirtazapine to oral venlafaxine 75 mg daily titrating over 4 weeks and review at 8 weeks
options.rationale: A third sequential antidepressant trial delays definitive treatment in a malnourished psychotically depressed older patient; ECT has superior response rate and speed. (Source: RANZCP: Mood disorders)

options.text: Add oral aripiprazole 5 mg daily to existing mirtazapine and continue for 8 weeks before reassessment
options.rationale: Antipsychotic augmentation is reasonable for non-psychotic treatment-resistant depression, but slow onset and inadequate efficacy in nihilistic delusions with malnutrition make ECT preferable here. (Source: RANZCP: Mood disorders)

options.text: Refer for bilateral electroconvulsive therapy after written informed consent and anaesthetic assessment
options.rationale: Psychotic depression in older adults responds rapidly to ECT and is the preferred next step when two adequate antidepressant trials have failed and there is malnutrition or risk to life. (Source: RANZCP: Mood disorders; eTG: Psychotropic)

options.text: Commence oral lithium 250 mg twice daily for augmentation aiming for level 0.6 to 0.8 mmol/L
options.rationale: Lithium augmentation in an older adult with reduced GFR and dehydration risk is high-risk and slow-acting, not the optimal next step here. (Source: RANZCP: Mood disorders)

options.text: Refer for transcranial magnetic stimulation as outpatient over the next 6 weeks
options.rationale: TMS lacks adequate evidence in psychotic depression and is generally avoided in this subgroup; inpatient ECT is preferred. (Source: RANZCP: Mood disorders)

explanation.summary: Major depression with psychotic features in older adults carries a high mortality from suicide and physical decline. Adequate antidepressant trial is at least 6 to 8 weeks at therapeutic dose. After two such failures, particularly with malnutrition or active suicide risk, ECT is the preferred next step with response rates of 70 to 90% within 6 to 12 sessions. Capacity should be assessed and informed consent obtained; involuntary treatment under the relevant Mental Health Act may apply when capacity is lost.
key_points: Two adequate antidepressant trials precede ECT in non-emergent cases.
Psychotic depression with malnutrition or suicide risk justifies earlier ECT.
Capacity assessment and informed consent precede ECT where possible.
explanation.pearls: Older adult with psychotic depression refusing food after 2 antidepressant failures should be offered ECT within days; expected response rate is 70 to 90% in 6 to 12 sessions.

stem: You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar reviewing a 22-year-old woman admitted with mutism, immobility and refusal to eat or drink for 4 days. She has a first presentation of a probable mood disorder. Medical workup including computed tomography of the brain, lumbar puncture, autoimmune encephalitis panel and toxicology has been unremarkable. An intramuscular lorazepam 2 mg trial 48 hours ago produced a brief partial improvement that lasted 2 hours. She has since had two further 2 mg doses without sustained benefit. She has developed pressure areas and has stopped passing urine.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/86 mmHg, HR 110 bpm, T 37.4°C, RR 18, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale 28 of 69, mutism, posturing, waxy flexibility, no rigidity, no rash
data_table.Investigations: Creatine kinase 480 U/L, Na 134 mmol/L, K 4.2 mmol/L, creatinine 110 micromol/L, lactate 1.6 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence intravenous amantadine 200 mg twice daily as the next step in management while continuing lorazepam
options.rationale: NMDA antagonists are an unproven third-line option after benzodiazepines and ECT have failed; they should not displace ECT in this presentation. (Source: RANZCP: ECT)

options.text: Increase lorazepam to 8 mg orally over 24 hours and continue for a further 5 days before considering ECT referral
options.rationale: Lorazepam non-response within 48 to 72 hours alongside complications mandates escalation to ECT rather than further dose increases; prolonged immobility risks rhabdomyolysis and DVT. (Source: RANZCP: ECT)

options.text: Commence oral olanzapine 10 mg daily as the next step in management and re-evaluate at 7 days for response
options.rationale: Antipsychotics can worsen catatonia and may precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome; they are generally avoided until catatonia has resolved. (Source: RANZCP: ECT)

options.text: Refer for bilateral electroconvulsive therapy starting within 24 hours with daily anaesthetic and physical review
options.rationale: Catatonia not responding to a lorazepam challenge requires prompt ECT, particularly when there is dehydration, immobility complications and creatine kinase elevation suggesting evolving malignant catatonia. (Source: RANZCP: ECT; eTG: Psychotropic)

options.text: Stop all psychotropic medications and continue supportive care for 7 days before reassessment of catatonic features
options.rationale: Watchful waiting in a dehydrated immobile patient with rising CK risks progression to malignant catatonia with multi-organ failure. (Source: RANZCP: ECT)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a transdiagnostic syndrome of mutism, immobility, posturing, waxy flexibility, mutism and stupor. The Bush-Francis scale screens and grades severity. First-line treatment is a lorazepam challenge of 1 to 2 mg with response monitored; titration up to 8 to 24 mg daily may be needed. ECT is indicated when benzodiazepines fail, when malignant catatonia evolves with autonomic instability and elevated CK, or when life-threatening complications such as dehydration emerge. Antipsychotics may precipitate or worsen catatonia and are avoided until resolution.
key_points: Bush-Francis screening confirms catatonia; lorazepam challenge is first-line.
ECT is the next step when benzodiazepine fails or malignant catatonia evolves.
Avoid antipsychotics during active catatonia.
explanation.pearls: When a lorazepam challenge gives only transient benefit and CK rises with autonomic instability, malignant catatonia is evolving and ECT should start within 24 hours.

stem: You are the ED registrar in a regional hospital 220 km from the nearest cardiac catheterisation laboratory. A 58-year-old man arrives with 80 minutes of central crushing chest pain radiating to his left arm. He has hypertension and dyslipidaemia and is a current smoker. He took aspirin 300 mg on the ambulance and is haemodynamically stable. The nearest PCI service has confirmed a minimum 150-minute door-to-balloon time including air transfer.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/86 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, RR 18, SpO2 96% on room air, T 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, chest clear, normal heart sounds, no murmur, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: 12-lead ECG: 2 mm ST elevation V2-V4 with reciprocal changes in II, III, aVF; troponin pending; no prior ECG available

lead_in: What is the most appropriate reperfusion strategy?

options.text: Commence intravenous metoprolol 5 mg three times to slow heart rate and defer reperfusion until troponin returns
options.rationale: Beta-blockers in STEMI are timed after reperfusion and not before; waiting for troponin delays time-critical therapy. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ: STEMI)

options.text: Administer intravenous heparin alone and arrange transfer for primary PCI on arrival 150 minutes after first medical contact
options.rationale: Delaying reperfusion to 150 minutes when fibrinolysis can be given within 30 minutes loses myocardium; fibrinolysis is preferred when PCI cannot be delivered within 120 minutes. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ: STEMI)

options.text: Continue oral aspirin 300 mg only and arrange transfer for angiography within 24 hours without acute reperfusion
options.rationale: Antiplatelet therapy alone does not deliver reperfusion in STEMI; either PCI or fibrinolysis is required. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ: STEMI)

options.text: Administer oral ticagrelor 180 mg load and observe in the coronary care unit without further reperfusion therapy
options.rationale: Ticagrelor is an adjunct after the reperfusion decision and does not substitute for fibrinolysis or PCI. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ: STEMI)

options.text: Administer intravenous tenecteplase weight-based bolus plus clopidogrel and enoxaparin then transfer for early angiography within 24 hours
options.rationale: When PCI cannot be delivered within 120 minutes of first medical contact, fibrinolysis with tenecteplase plus adjunctive clopidogrel and enoxaparin followed by pharmaco-invasive transfer is the recommended Australian pathway. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ: STEMI; eTG: Cardiovascular)

explanation.summary: STEMI reperfusion strategy depends on time from symptom onset and door-to-balloon delay. Primary PCI is preferred when it can be delivered within 120 minutes of first medical contact. Otherwise, fibrinolysis with tenecteplase is given within 30 minutes of arrival, combined with aspirin, clopidogrel (preferred over prasugrel and ticagrelor pre-fibrinolysis) and enoxaparin, then transfer for early angiography within 3 to 24 hours. Contraindications to fibrinolysis include recent intracranial bleed, ischaemic stroke within 3 months, severe hypertension and active internal bleeding.
key_points: PCI is preferred if achievable within 120 minutes; otherwise fibrinolysis within 30 minutes.
Adjuncts post-fibrinolysis: aspirin, clopidogrel, enoxaparin.
Pharmaco-invasive transfer for angiography is undertaken within 3 to 24 hours.
explanation.pearls: When predicted door-to-balloon exceeds 120 minutes, give intravenous tenecteplase within 30 minutes of arrival plus clopidogrel 300 mg and enoxaparin, then transfer for early angiography.

stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 54-year-old man admitted overnight with epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea and vomiting. He drinks 80 g of alcohol most evenings. He has been started on intravenous fluids and morphine. At 24 hours after admission you are asked to reassess him because he has become more tachypnoeic, his urine output has fallen and he feels worse. He has no history of cardiac or renal disease.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 102/64 mmHg, HR 116 bpm, RR 28, SpO2 92% on 4 L oxygen via nasal prongs, T 38.2°C
data_table.Examination: Abdomen diffusely tender with guarding, no peritonism, no rebound, no rash, no jaundice, urine output 18 mL/hour over the last 3 hours
data_table.Investigations: Lipase 1820 U/L, calcium 1.92 mmol/L, glucose 11.4 mmol/L, urea 14.6 mmol/L, WCC 18.4 x10^9/L, LDH 480 U/L, AST 220 U/L, PaO2 on arterial gas 58 mmHg on supplemental oxygen

lead_in: Which severity stratification action is most appropriate now?

options.text: Apply Ranson criteria at 48 hours and continue current ward-level management until that time point
options.rationale: Ranson criteria are designed for prognostic scoring not real-time triage and delay critical care decisions; clinical and physiological assessment must guide escalation now. (Source: GESA: Acute pancreatitis)

options.text: Commence intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics prophylactically to prevent infected necrosis
options.rationale: Prophylactic antibiotics do not reduce mortality or infected necrosis in acute pancreatitis and are not recommended; antibiotics are reserved for documented infection. (Source: GESA: Acute pancreatitis)

options.text: Continue ward-level care with intravenous fluids and morphine and review at 48 hours with repeat lipase
options.rationale: Repeat lipase has no prognostic value and ward-level care is inadequate for a patient meeting organ failure criteria. (Source: GESA: Acute pancreatitis)

options.text: Arrange urgent endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography to relieve presumed gallstone obstruction
options.rationale: ERCP in acute pancreatitis is reserved for cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction; this presentation is alcohol-related and ERCP is not indicated. (Source: GESA: Acute pancreatitis)

options.text: Transfer urgently to a high-dependency unit because the patient meets organ failure criteria for severe acute pancreatitis
options.rationale: Persistent organ failure beyond 48 hours defines severe acute pancreatitis; tachypnoea with PaO2 under 60 mmHg, oliguria and tachycardia in this patient represent at least respiratory and cardiovascular dysfunction warranting HDU-level care. (Source: GESA: Acute pancreatitis; ANZICS guidance)

explanation.summary: Severity stratification of acute pancreatitis uses the revised Atlanta classification: mild (no organ failure), moderately severe (transient organ failure or local complications) and severe (persistent organ failure beyond 48 hours). Modified Marshall scoring identifies cardiovascular, respiratory and renal dysfunction. Severe disease has 20 to 50% mortality and demands HDU or ICU level care, early enteral nutrition and CT imaging only after 72 hours unless complications are suspected. Prophylactic antibiotics are not supported.
key_points: Persistent organ failure beyond 48 hours defines severe acute pancreatitis.
Modified Marshall score grades respiratory, cardiovascular and renal dysfunction.
Avoid prophylactic antibiotics; reserve CT for after 72 hours.
explanation.pearls: Severe acute pancreatitis is defined by persistent organ failure beyond 48 hours; PaO2 under 60 mmHg, urine output under 0.5 mL/kg/hour or systolic BP under 90 mmHg trigger HDU transfer.

stem: You are the neurology registrar reviewing a 68-year-old man in the stroke clinic 3 days after a transient ischaemic attack of right-sided weakness and aphasia lasting 30 minutes that resolved spontaneously. He has hypertension, hyperlipidaemia and is a former smoker. He is on aspirin 100 mg daily and has been started on atorvastatin 80 mg daily. He has no atrial fibrillation on Holter monitoring. His ABCD2 score is 5.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, T 36.7°C, sinus rhythm
data_table.Examination: Normal neurological examination, no carotid bruits audible, no signs of cardiac failure
data_table.Investigations: Carotid duplex: 80% stenosis left internal carotid artery, MRI brain DWI shows small acute left frontal infarct, no haemorrhage, lipids LDL 1.8 mmol/L on statin, HbA1c 6.0%

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.text: Add clopidogrel 75 mg daily as long-term dual antiplatelet therapy without carotid surgical or stenting intervention
options.rationale: Long-term DAPT has not been shown to substitute for revascularisation in symptomatic high-grade carotid stenosis and increases bleeding risk. (Source: NSF Stroke)

options.text: Continue medical management alone with aspirin and atorvastatin and arrange surveillance carotid duplex in 6 months
options.rationale: Surgical revascularisation is superior to medical management alone for symptomatic 70 to 99% stenosis; medical-only management is reserved for asymptomatic or surgically high-risk patients. (Source: NSF Stroke)

options.text: Refer for carotid endarterectomy ideally within 2 weeks of the symptomatic event with ongoing aspirin and statin therapy
options.rationale: Symptomatic carotid stenosis between 70% and 99% benefits most from carotid endarterectomy within 14 days of the index event; benefit declines markedly beyond 4 weeks. (Source: NSF Stroke; eTG: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Refer for carotid endarterectomy electively in 3 to 6 months once the patient has fully recovered from the TIA event
options.rationale: Delaying surgery beyond 14 days reduces the absolute risk reduction in stroke; the highest recurrence risk falls in the first 2 weeks. (Source: NSF Stroke)

options.text: Refer for carotid artery stenting as first-line intervention in this 68-year-old man with high-grade symptomatic stenosis
options.rationale: Carotid endarterectomy remains the standard for symptomatic stenosis in patients over 70 with suitable anatomy; stenting is reserved for surgically high-risk patients or unfavourable anatomy. (Source: NSF Stroke)

explanation.summary: Symptomatic carotid stenosis between 70% and 99% benefits substantially from carotid endarterectomy, with the largest absolute risk reduction when surgery occurs within 14 days of the index event. Number needed to treat to prevent one stroke at 5 years is approximately 6 when surgery happens within 2 weeks but rises sharply with delay. Best medical therapy with aspirin, statin and blood pressure control continues alongside surgery. Carotid artery stenting is an alternative when endarterectomy is contraindicated.
key_points: Symptomatic carotid stenosis 70 to 99% should be operated within 14 days.
Number needed to treat is approximately 6 within 2 weeks and rises with delay.
Continue antiplatelet, statin and BP control alongside surgery.
explanation.pearls: TIA with ipsilateral 70 to 99% carotid stenosis - book carotid endarterectomy within 2 weeks; the absolute risk reduction halves if surgery is delayed beyond 4 weeks.

stem: You are the cardiology registrar reviewing a 68-year-old man with newly diagnosed heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. His echocardiogram shows LVEF 28% and a moderately dilated left ventricle. He has type 2 diabetes, hypertension and chronic kidney disease stage 3a. He is currently on ramipril 5 mg daily, frusemide 40 mg daily and metformin 1 g twice daily. He has been improving over 3 days of hospital admission and is now euvolaemic.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 98/58 mmHg seated, HR 72 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air, T 36.6°C
data_table.Examination: JVP not raised, no peripheral oedema, lungs clear, mild left ventricular heave, no murmur
data_table.Investigations: K 4.6 mmol/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L (eGFR 46 mL/min/1.73 m^2), HbA1c 7.2%, NT-proBNP 1800 pg/mL

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next medication adjustment?

options.text: Increase ramipril to 10 mg daily aiming for maximum tolerated ACE inhibitor dose
options.rationale: Up-titration of ramipril risks hypotension in a patient with SBP 98 mmHg; further BP-neutral pillars should be added before pushing the ACE inhibitor. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ: Heart failure)

options.text: Switch ramipril to sacubitril-valsartan 24/26 mg twice daily today
options.rationale: Sacubitril-valsartan introduces additional blood pressure-lowering effect and is uncertain in this borderline normotensive patient until SGLT2 inhibitor is established; transition usually requires SBP above 100 mmHg. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ: Heart failure)

options.text: Add spironolactone 25 mg daily today as the next pillar of guideline-directed therapy
options.rationale: MRA addition is appropriate but with current eGFR 46 and potassium 4.6 mmol/L it requires close electrolyte monitoring; SGLT2 inhibition has a more favourable risk profile here as the immediate addition. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ: Heart failure)

options.text: Add empagliflozin 10 mg daily and continue ramipril 5 mg daily without dose changes
options.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitors provide mortality benefit in HFrEF independent of diabetes status, are tolerated down to eGFR 20 mL/min and do not lower blood pressure significantly when added to background therapy; this is the next step in low-BP HFrEF optimisation. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ: Heart failure; AMH)

options.text: Add ivabradine 5 mg twice daily for heart rate control alongside the current therapy
options.rationale: Ivabradine requires sinus rhythm with heart rate above 75 bpm despite maximal beta-blocker therapy; this patient is not yet on a beta-blocker. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ: Heart failure)

explanation.summary: The four pillars of HFrEF therapy are ACE inhibitors or ARNI, beta-blockers, MRAs and SGLT2 inhibitors. Modern practice favours rapid sequential introduction of all four pillars at low dose rather than full up-titration of each before adding the next. SGLT2 inhibitors have minimal blood pressure effect and are favoured in low-BP patients. ARNI introduction usually requires SBP above 100 mmHg and a 36-hour ACE inhibitor washout before the first sacubitril-valsartan dose.
key_points: Sequential low-dose introduction of all four HFrEF pillars beats slow uptitration of each.
SGLT2 inhibitors are BP-neutral and effective down to eGFR 20 mL/min.
ARNI introduction generally needs SBP above 100 mmHg and 36-hour ACE inhibitor washout.
explanation.pearls: In low-BP HFrEF the SGLT2 inhibitor empagliflozin or dapagliflozin can be added without dose changes to other pillars and offers mortality benefit within weeks.

stem: You are the infectious diseases registrar reviewing a 38-year-old man newly diagnosed with HIV through community testing. He has a CD4 count of 78 cells/microL and viral load of 350,000 copies/mL. He has no current symptoms of headache, fever or visual changes. As part of baseline workup his serum cryptococcal antigen has returned positive at a titre of 1:160. He has not commenced antiretroviral therapy yet. He has no medication allergies and is not on any current medications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/74 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.7°C, weight 72 kg
data_table.Examination: No meningism, no focal neurology, normal fundoscopy, no skin lesions, no oral candidiasis, no lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: Serum cryptococcal antigen positive 1:160, lumbar puncture pending, MRI brain pending, FBC, UEC, LFTs within normal limits, hepatitis serology negative, tuberculin IGRA negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Defer all interventions and repeat serum CrAg in 4 weeks to see if the titre declines spontaneously
options.rationale: Untreated cryptococcal infection in advanced HIV progresses to disseminated disease and meningitis; intervention cannot wait. (Source: ASHM: HIV)

options.text: Commence antiretroviral therapy with bictegravir-emtricitabine-tenofovir alafenamide and review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Starting ART without first excluding cryptococcal meningitis risks fatal cryptococcal IRIS within weeks of immune reconstitution. (Source: ASHM: HIV)

options.text: Commence oral fluconazole 800 mg daily for 2 weeks then start ART without lumbar puncture
options.rationale: Oral fluconazole alone is inadequate for cryptococcal meningitis induction therapy if disease is present; LP must be performed to know which regimen is required. (Source: ASHM: HIV)

options.text: Perform lumbar puncture today to exclude cryptococcal meningitis before any treatment decision
options.rationale: Asymptomatic serum CrAg positivity in advanced HIV requires lumbar puncture to identify subclinical meningitis, which would mandate induction antifungal therapy with delayed ART start to mitigate IRIS. (Source: ASHM: HIV; BHIVA guidance)

options.text: Refer for primary prophylaxis with oral itraconazole 200 mg daily without further investigation
options.rationale: Itraconazole is not the recommended cryptococcal prophylaxis or treatment agent and does not replace evaluation for CNS involvement. (Source: ASHM: HIV)

explanation.summary: In advanced HIV with CD4 under 100 cells/microL, serum cryptococcal antigen screening identifies subclinical infection. A positive result requires lumbar puncture to exclude cryptococcal meningitis. If meningitis is present, induction antifungal therapy combines intravenous liposomal amphotericin B and flucytosine for 2 weeks, followed by consolidation with fluconazole and maintenance. ART is delayed by 4 to 6 weeks after antifungal initiation to reduce cryptococcal IRIS risk.
key_points: Serum CrAg-positive HIV patients with CD4 under 100 cells/microL need LP regardless of symptoms.
Defer ART by 4 to 6 weeks after antifungal start to reduce cryptococcal IRIS risk.
Liposomal amphotericin B plus flucytosine is the induction regimen for cryptococcal meningitis.
explanation.pearls: Asymptomatic serum CrAg-positive with CD4 under 100 cells/microL needs LP first; if meningitis is confirmed delay ART by 4 to 6 weeks after antifungal induction.

stem: You are the medical registrar in resus seeing a 28-year-old woman with known generalised epilepsy on lamotrigine, brought in with a generalised tonic-clonic seizure that started 12 minutes before arrival and has continued. Paramedics gave 10 mg intramuscular midazolam without termination. On arrival she received a further intravenous 4 mg lorazepam without effect. She is not pregnant on point-of-care testing. Her airway is patent on a jaw thrust and she has IV access.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/96 mmHg, HR 138 bpm, RR 24, SpO2 94% on 15 L oxygen via non-rebreather, T 37.8°C, BSL 5.8 mmol/L
data_table.Examination: Ongoing generalised tonic-clonic seizure, no focal neurology, no rash, no head injury, no needle marks
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.22 with metabolic acidosis, lactate 6.8 mmol/L, Na 138 mmol/L, lamotrigine level pending, urine drug screen pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Intravenous levetiracetam 60 mg/kg infused over 5 to 10 minutes with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: After two adequate doses of benzodiazepine in established status epilepticus, intravenous levetiracetam 60 mg/kg is the preferred second-line option with rapid onset, broad efficacy and no drug interactions. (Source: NSF Status epilepticus; eTG: Neurology)

options.text: Intravenous phenytoin 20 mg/kg infused over 30 minutes with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Phenytoin remains an alternative but ESETT-equivalent trial data show levetiracetam achieves seizure cessation at similar rates with fewer cardiovascular side effects in young adults. (Source: NSF Status epilepticus)

options.text: Intravenous propofol 2 mg/kg bolus and infusion to induce general anaesthesia immediately
options.rationale: Anaesthesia is third-line therapy after a failed second-line antiseizure medication; jumping to anaesthesia denies the chance of terminating with a non-anaesthetic agent. (Source: NSF Status epilepticus)

options.text: Intravenous sodium valproate 40 mg/kg infused over 10 minutes with electrolyte monitoring
options.rationale: Sodium valproate is acceptable but is generally avoided in women of childbearing potential due to teratogenicity; levetiracetam is preferred in this group. (Source: NSF Status epilepticus)

options.text: Intravenous midazolam 0.2 mg/kg as a third benzodiazepine dose to attempt termination
options.rationale: After two adequate benzodiazepine doses the failure rate of a third dose is high and respiratory depression accumulates; the standard next step is second-line antiseizure medication. (Source: NSF Status epilepticus)

explanation.summary: Status epilepticus is defined as a seizure lasting longer than 5 minutes or recurrent seizures without recovery between. First-line therapy is benzodiazepine: lorazepam 4 mg IV or midazolam 10 mg IM as a single repeatable dose. After two adequate doses without termination, second-line antiseizure medication is given. Australian and ESETT trial data support levetiracetam, sodium valproate or phenytoin with similar efficacy; levetiracetam is preferred in pregnancy, lactation and young women of childbearing potential.
key_points: Two benzodiazepine doses are first-line; a third is not recommended.
Second-line: levetiracetam 60 mg/kg, sodium valproate 40 mg/kg or phenytoin 20 mg/kg.
Levetiracetam is preferred in women of childbearing potential.
explanation.pearls: After two failed benzodiazepine doses in status epilepticus, intravenous levetiracetam 60 mg/kg over 10 minutes is the preferred second-line in women of childbearing potential.

stem: You are the medical registrar planning a non-emergency CT coronary angiogram in a 74-year-old man with stable angina to clarify the cause of his recurrent chest pain. He has type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease stage 3b with stable eGFR 32, and well-controlled hypertension. He is on perindopril, frusemide 40 mg daily, atorvastatin and metformin. He is not in acute heart failure and has had no recent illness. The cardiologist has requested your input on peri-procedural medication.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 128/74 mmHg, HR 68 bpm, T 36.6°C, weight 84 kg
data_table.Examination: JVP not raised, no peripheral oedema, lungs clear, no abdominal tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Creatinine 168 micromol/L, eGFR 32 mL/min/1.73 m^2, K 4.4 mmol/L, HbA1c 6.8%, urine ACR 14 mg/mmol

lead_in: Which combination of peri-procedural strategies is most appropriate?

options.text: Give intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1.4% at 3 mL/kg/hour for 1 hour pre-procedure as the sole preventive intervention
options.rationale: Sodium bicarbonate has not been shown to be superior to sodium chloride 0.9% and is no longer routinely recommended for contrast-associated AKI prevention. (Source: KHA-CARI; AMH)

options.text: Withhold metformin permanently and give oral N-acetylcysteine 1200 mg twice daily for 24 hours pre- and post-procedure
options.rationale: Permanent metformin cessation is unnecessary and oral N-acetylcysteine has been shown to have no benefit in preventing contrast-associated AKI. (Source: KHA-CARI; AMH)

options.text: Continue all current medications including perindopril, frusemide and metformin without peri-procedural changes
options.rationale: Diuretics increase intravascular volume depletion and metformin should be withheld when eGFR is under 45 mL/min to avoid lactic acidosis if AKI develops. (Source: KHA-CARI; AMH)

options.text: Withhold metformin for 48 hours from morning of procedure, give intravenous sodium chloride 0.9% at 1 mL/kg/hour pre and post
options.rationale: Patients with eGFR under 45 mL/min undergoing iodinated contrast warrant volume expansion with isotonic saline, minimum contrast volume and temporary metformin withdrawal to prevent lactic acidosis from procedural AKI. (Source: KHA-CARI; AMH)

options.text: Administer prophylactic intravenous loop diuretic 80 mg immediately pre-contrast to enhance contrast clearance
options.rationale: Diuretic-induced volume depletion worsens contrast nephropathy risk and is contraindicated as prophylaxis. (Source: KHA-CARI; AMH)

explanation.summary: Contrast-associated acute kidney injury risk rises with reduced eGFR, diabetes, heart failure, hypovolaemia and use of nephrotoxic drugs. The most consistently effective preventive strategy is intravenous isotonic crystalloid hydration before and after contrast administration. Metformin is held for 48 hours when eGFR is under 45 mL/min because of the risk of metformin-associated lactic acidosis if AKI develops. Iso-osmolar or low-osmolar contrast at the smallest necessary volume reduces risk further. N-acetylcysteine and sodium bicarbonate are not routinely recommended.
key_points: Intravenous sodium chloride 0.9% pre- and post-procedure is the primary intervention.
Hold metformin for 48 hours when eGFR is under 45 mL/min.
N-acetylcysteine and sodium bicarbonate are no longer recommended.
explanation.pearls: For eGFR 30 to 45 mL/min undergoing iodinated contrast, intravenous saline at 1 mL/kg/hour for 12 hours pre- and post-procedure with 48-hour metformin pause is the preventive standard.

stem: You are the endocrine registrar planning the surgical preparation for a 47-year-old woman with a 5.2 cm right adrenal mass producing markedly elevated 24-hour fractionated metanephrines. She has paroxysmal severe headaches with palpitations and pallor. Her resting blood pressure is 168/108 mmHg with episodes recorded to 220/120 mmHg during paroxysms. Her HbA1c is 6.4% and she is not on any current medications. Cardiology review showed normal left ventricular function. Surgery is planned for 14 days from now.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/108 mmHg average over the last day, HR 96 bpm, RR 16, T 36.6°C, weight 64 kg
data_table.Examination: Patient anxious, mild diaphoresis at rest, no signs of cardiac failure, no abdominal mass palpable
data_table.Investigations: Plasma metanephrines markedly elevated, 24-hour urinary metanephrines elevated, K 3.6 mmol/L, Mg 0.78 mmol/L, ECG sinus tachycardia, MRI confirms 5.2 cm right adrenal lesion

lead_in: Which preoperative regimen is most appropriate?

options.text: Phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily titrated against orthostatic BP, add atenolol 25 mg daily after 7 days, high salt diet
options.rationale: The Endocrine Society Australia-aligned regimen starts alpha-blockade 10 to 14 days before surgery, adds a beta-blocker only after adequate alpha-blockade to avoid unopposed alpha-mediated hypertensive crisis, and uses sodium loading to expand intravascular volume before tumour resection. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia; eTG: Endocrinology)

options.text: Atenolol 25 mg daily first to control heart rate then add phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily after 5 days, normal salt diet
options.rationale: Starting beta-blockade before adequate alpha-blockade leaves unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction and can precipitate a hypertensive crisis. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia)

options.text: Amlodipine 5 mg daily titrated to control blood pressure as the sole preoperative agent for 14 days before surgery
options.rationale: Calcium channel blockers are second-line additions when alpha-blockade alone is insufficient; they are not preferred monotherapy because they do not blunt catecholamine surge during tumour manipulation. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia)

options.text: Proceed directly to surgery without preoperative blockade because the patient has been asymptomatic between paroxysms
options.rationale: Unprepared phaeochromocytoma surgery carries 25 to 50% perioperative mortality from cardiovascular collapse and is contraindicated. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate infusion alone for 14 days before surgery as the sole preoperative blockade agent
options.rationale: Magnesium is a useful intraoperative adjunct but is not the recommended preoperative blockade regimen. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia)

explanation.summary: Phaeochromocytoma preoperative preparation aims to prevent intraoperative hypertensive crisis, arrhythmia and post-resection hypotension. Alpha-blockade is started 10 to 14 days before surgery with phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin titrated against orthostatic blood pressure. Once adequate alpha-blockade is achieved, a beta-blocker can be added to control reflex tachycardia. Sodium and water loading expands intravascular volume to prevent post-resection hypotension. Intraoperative adjuncts include magnesium sulfate, sodium nitroprusside and short-acting beta-blockers.
key_points: Alpha-blockade precedes beta-blockade by at least 7 days.
Phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin titrated against orthostatic BP.
Sodium loading expands intravascular volume for post-resection BP support.
explanation.pearls: Start phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily 10 to 14 days before phaeochromocytoma resection and only add a beta-blocker once alpha-blockade is established; reverse sequence risks fatal unopposed alpha-mediated crisis.
